Facts.text: The University of Leipzig opens
English language newspaper "Namloos" begins publishing in Amsterdam
The second Eddystone Lighthouse on Eddystone Rocks off English southern coast is destroyed by fire
Touro shul of Newport, Rhode Island dedicated (oldest existing US synagogue)
British General Howe plots attack on Washington's army for Dec 4
Austrian army occupies Brussels
Britain sells Suriname to the Netherlands
Napoleon defeats Russians & Austrians at Austerlitz
First savings bank in US opens (Philadelphia Savings Fund Society)
In San Salvador, a congress proposes incorporation into the United States
Gaetano Donizetti's opera "La Favorita" premieres in Paris
French President Charles Louis Bonaparte in a Coup d'etat overthrows the Second Republic establishing himself as Emperor.
Skirmish at Rocky Creek Church, Georgia
Amsterdam Artis Zoo opens its aquarium
Opera "Regina di Saba" premieres in Vienna
Amsterdam's Oscar Carrés Circus Theater opens
French president Jules Grévy resigns

International Baseball League disbands; teams in Syracuse, Toronto, Hamilton, and Buffalo form the International Association; those in Newark, Jersey City, Wilkes-Barre, and Scranton become the Central League
52nd US Congress (1891-93) convenes
54th US Congress (1895-97) convenes
Philippine–American War: The Battle of Tirad Pass, termed "The Filipino Thermopylae", is fought.
US & Germany agree to divide Samoa between them
Dutch soccer club Be Quick - later Go Ahead, forms in Deventer; suffix 'Eagles' added in 1971
Association of Football Players' and Trainers' Union (English Professional Football Players' Association) is formed by Charlie Roberts and Billy Meredith in Manchester, England
Child Emperor Pu Yi ascends the Chinese throne at the age of two

National Hockey Association (NHA) is formed in Montreal; original members include Montreal Wanderers and Montreal Canadiens; becomes NHL after some NHA teams leave due to ownership disagreements, and create their own league
French Government of Louis Barthou falls due to overtime conscription
Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Managua created in Nicaragua
Austria army occupies Belgrade, Serbia
National Baseball Commission orders that injured players get full pay for duration of their contracts; injury clause previously let clubs suspend players after 15 days' pay
Han Yong-woon, finds Zen awakening at Osean Monastery, Korea
British-German trade agreement signed
1st Model A Ford sold, for only $385
First skull of Peking man found in the caves of Zhoukoudian, 50 km outside of Peking, China. Later dated roughly 750,000 years old.
"Adventures of Charlie Chan" 1st heard on NBC-Blue radio network

Australia ends Day 1 of controversial 'Bodyline' cricket series v England at 290/6 in Sydney; Stan McCabe 127no (finishes at 187no); notorious England fast bowler Harold Larwood takes 4 wickets; England wins by 10 wickets
5.08-m (200") Mt Palomar Observatory mirror is cast
The first 'Kindertransport' carrying Jewish refugee children from Nazi Germany arrives in Britain
British Imperial Airways & British Airways merge to form BOAC
NY's La Guardia Airport began operations as an airliner from Chicago lands, 1 minute after midnight
Largest roller skating rink (outside of NYC) opens in Peekskill, New York
US Naval Intelligence ceases bugging Japanese consul
First RSHA (Reichssicherheitshauptamt - Nazi Reich Security Head Office) transport out of Vienna reaches Birkenau camp
10th Heisman Trophy Award: Les Horvath, Ohio State (QB)
German troops seize dikles in Betuwse, Netherland
US 95th Infantry division occupies bridge at Saar, Germany
13th Heisman Trophy Award: Johnny Lujack, Notre Dame (QB)

South African world bantamweight boxing champion Vic Toweel sets a record for knockdowns in a title fight against Englishman Danny Sullivan in Johannesburg; Sullivan floored 14 times in 10 rounds before fight stopped
Philadelphia Eagles set NFL record of 25 1st-downs rushing in 35-21 win over Washington Redskins at Griffith Stadium
18th Heisman Trophy Award: Billy Vessels, Oklahoma (HB)
First human birth televised to public on KOA-TV Denver, Colorado
Milwaukee Hawks shooting guard Frank Selvey sets then NBA record of 24 of 26 free throws as he scores 42 points in 117-108 win over the Minneapolis Lakers at Milwaukee Arena
Taiwan (ROC) & US sign the Mutual Defense Treaty, preventing Mainland China (PRC) from taking Taiwan between 1955-1979
US Senate censures Joeseph McCarthy (Sen-R-Wisc) for "conduct that tends to bring Senate into dishonor & disrepute"
1st US large scale nuclear power plant for peacetime use opens, Shippingport, Pennsylvania
NFL Draft: Rice quarterback King Hill #1 pick by Chicago Cardinals

Pacific Coast League franchises forced to relocate when Dodgers and Giants confirm their move to California for 1958 MLB season; Hollywood Stars move from LA to Salt Lake City; LA Angels move to Spokane, and San Francisco Seals transfer to Phoenix
1959 NFL Draft: Randy Duncan QB University of Iowa #1 pick by Green Bay Packers
24th Heisman Trophy Award: Pete Dawkins, Army (HB)
Benelux treaty signed by Belgium, Netherlands & Luxembourg
KNOP TV channel 2 in North Platte, NB (NBC) begins broadcasting
Malpasset dam collapses destroying French Riviera town of Frejus
Fidel Castrodeclares he's a Marxist & will lead Cuba to Communism
Wind Bell, journal of San Francisco Zen Center, begins publishing
1964 NFL Draft: Dave Parks from Texas Tech first pick by San Francisco 49ers
First Dutch rocket launched, reaches height of 10 km
MLB Rules Committee bans oversized catcher's mitts; rule effective only in 1965
Boeing 747 jumbo jet 1st public preview (Seattle to NYC)
Two years after doubling its size from six to 12, the NHL announces 2 new teams would be joining the League the following year; Buffalo Sabres and Vancouver Canucks
British composer Michael Tippett's opera "The Knot Garden" premieres at the Royal Opera House, Covent Garden, London, England
Environmental Protection Agency begins in the US under Director William Ruckelshaus
Soviet space probe Mars 3 is first to soft land on Mars
Trucial States Abu Dhabi, Ajman, Dubai, Fujairah, Sharjah and Umm Al Quwain form the United Arab Emirates and declare independence from the United Kingdom
"December Giant" largest sinkhole in US collapses (Alabama)

USC tailback Anthony Davis becomes first to score 6 touchdowns against Notre Dame; in a 45-23 victory over the Fighting Irish Davis at the LA Coliseum he scores on runs of 1, 4, 5 & 8 yards and 2 kickoff returns
Capital Centre (USAir Arena) opens in Landover Maryland; NBA's Washington Bullets beat Seattle SuperSonics, 98-96 in opening game; arena demolished 2002
MLB Rules Committee changes long time philosophy to permit the use of cowhide, rather than just horsehide in the manufacture of baseballs
Soyuz 16 launched into Earth orbit for 6 days
7 South Moluccans hijack train at Wijster, Drente (NL), 3 killed
Laos king Sisavang Vatthana resigns, Lao People's Democratic Republic founded (National Day)
Communist revolutionaryFidel Castrobecomes President of Cuba, replacing Osvaldo Dorticós Torrado
Protesters chant "Allah is great" at an Anti-Shah protest in Tehran, Iran
The Sporting News announces Baltimore Orioles shortstop Mark Belanger winner of his 8th (and final) MLB Gold Glove Award
Crowds attack US embassy in Tripoli, Libya
Foots Walker becomes 1st Cleveland Cavalier to score a triple-double
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
4 American Maryknoll nuns killed by death squads in El Salvador
Moscone Convention Center, San Francisco, opens at 11:30 AM
Spanish government requests membership of NATO

1st permanent artificial heart successfully implanted in retired dentist Barney Clark (61) of Seattle at the University of Utah Hospital by cardiothoracic surgeon William DeVries; Clark lived 112 days with Jarvik-7 heart

American track and field record holder Steve Scott sets record for fastest round of golf played on a regulation course; posts a 95, completing 18 holes in 29:33.05s at Miller Golf Club in Anaheim, California; he used only two clubs, and ran between holes
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan

Chicago Bears' head coachMike Ditkaand defensive coordinator Buddy Ryan almost come to blows at halftime in a 38-24 loss to the Miami Dolphins at the Orange Bowl, Miami; Chicago's only loss of the NFL season
NFL quarterback legendsDan Marinoand John Elway (Denver Broncos) face each other for the first time, a 30-26 win for Marino's Miami Dolphins (390 yards and 3 TDs)
Philipine Chief of Staff General Fabian speaks of Benigno Aquino's murder
Dow Jones Industrial Average hits record 1,955.57
"334" club forms as 334 brave Devil fans journey through 20" of snow to attend New Jersey Devils 7-5 NHL victory over Calgary at Meadowlands
Chicago City Council elects Eugene Sawyer acting mayor
Jennifer Steele, 17, of Colorado becomes Miss Teen of America [Approx]
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
"Naked Gun" movie based on TV's "Police Squad" premieres
5 gunmen who hijacked Soviet Aeroflot jet surrender in Israel
ESPN airs its 10,000th edition of 'Sports Center', making it the most televised cable program in history
STS-27 Atlantis launched (Secret military mission)
UN votes 151-2 (Israel and US) to move PLO debate to Geneva, Great Britain abstains
55th Heisman Trophy Award: Andre Ware, Houston (QB)
KHJ-TV in Los Angeles CA changes call letters to KCAL-TV
V. P. Singh sworn in as the 8th Prime Minister of India
First parliamentary election in newly reunified Germany
First time 12 people in space at the same time
US 69th manned space mission STS 35 (Columbia 11) launches into orbit
Australia routs India by 10 wickets in the 1st cricket Test in Brisbane; first Test to be officiated by a match referee; former England captain MJK Smith does the honours
Former Pittsburgh Pirates outfielder Bobby Bonilla becomes highest paid player in MLB when he agrees to record $29 million-5 year deal with New York Mets
Muslim Shi'ites release American hostage Joseph Cicippio held in Lebanon
Kiwi opening batsman John Wright becomes first New Zealander to make 5,000 runs in Test cricket when he scores 14 in the 2nd innings of the drawn 1st Test against Sri Lanka in Moratuwa
WQEW-AM radio replaces WQXR on 1560 in NYC
Dow Jones Industrial Average hits record 3702.11
Space shuttle STS-61 (Endeavour 5) launches
Andrew Lloyd Webberadmitted to hospital for ulcer treatment
Cruise liner Achille Lauro (Willem Ruys) sinks off the coast of Somalia
Jury finds Heidi Fleiss guilty of running a call girl ring

For only the second time in English Football League history, 5 players are sent from the field; 4 Bristol Rovers players and 1 from Wigan are dismissed in the Division 2 match at JJB Stadium; four of the red cards come in 45th minute
MCI Center opens in Washington, D.C., Wizards vs SuperSonics
The United Kingdom devolves political power in Northern Ireland to the Northern Ireland Executive

England holds off a determined comeback by South Africa to notch up their 3rd Southern Hemisphere rugby scalp of the autumn with a 25-17 win at Twickenham; previously beat Australia, 22-19 and Argentina, 19-0
Enron files for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in the Southern District of New York
New climate-controlled vault to conserve and display the Sarajevo Haggadah illuminated manuscript (c. 1350) dedicated in Sarajevo’s National Museum
Van Tuong Nguyen is executed in Singapore for drug trafficking.
Thai Prime Minister Somchai Wongsawat resigns after the 2008 Thailand political crisis
9 people are killed after 30 cars are trapped in Sasago Tunnel, Japan
Borut Pahor is elected President of Slovenia
Pier Luigi Bersani is elected Italian Prime Minister
China launches its first moon rover mission

After featuring in a magazine article with long-time life partner, veteran MLB umpire and crew chief Dale Scott openly acknowledges his homosexuality; overwhelmingly positive reaction from baseball circles
Hong Kong pro-democracy activists clash with police as they try to surround the government's headquarters in some of the worst violence since the protests began
The World Food Programme suspends critical food aid to more than 1.7 million Syrian refugees in Jordan, Lebanon, Turkey, Iraq and Egypt because of a lack of funds
Vanuatu police arrest at least 20 people over the deaths by hanging of two men accused of sorcery
Attack on a social services centre in San Bernardino, California kills 14 and wounds 17
Fire during a dance party at an illegally re=purposed warehouse in Oakland< California kills 36

For 6th time in 7 years, Cincinnati Reds 1st baseman Joey Votto wins Tip O'Neill Award as best Canadian MLB player; hit .326 with NL-leading .434 OBP, 29 HRs & 97 RBIs in an outstanding all-around season for last-place club
Egyptian lawyer Nabih al-Wahsh sentenced to 3 years in prison for saying women wearing ripped jeans should be raped
Hawaiian Carissa Moore earns her 4th World Surf League Women’s Championship Tour title with a third placing at the season-ending Maui Pro event at Honolua Bay
Steve Bullock and Joe Sestak drop out of the US Presidential race
The UK becomes the first western country to authorize a vaccine for COVID-19, the Pfizer/BioNTech vaccine
US Attorney General William Barr says there is no evidence of widespread voter fraud in the 2020 presidential election, despite claims by PresidentDonald Trump
US records its largest daily death toll for COVID-19 at 2,885 and for the first time patient numbers in hospital exceed 100,000
American rock band KISS concludes farewell "End of the Road" tour at Madison Square Garden, NYC; the encore is performed by the premiere of digital avatars of the group
Flooding and landslides in Northern Tanzania result in 63 deaths, amid ongoing massive El Nino flooding throughout East Africa
Anthony Ashley Cooper, 1st Earl of Shaftesbury flees to Amsterdam following the failure of his plots to prevent a Catholic succession should KingCharles IIdie
St Paul's Cathedral, designed by SirChristopher Wrenis consecrated for use (previous building destroyed in the Great Fire of London)
James Madisonre-elected President of the US, Elbridge Gerry Vice-President

US PresidentJames Monroedeclares the "Monroe Doctrine" opposing European colonialism in the Americas, arguing any European political intervention in the New World would be a hostile act against the United States
William Henry Harrisonelected the 9th President of the United States of America
US PresidentJames K. Polkannounces to Congress that the United States should aggressively expand into the West, a widely held belief termed "manifest destiny" by newspaper editor John O'Sullivan
Franz Joseph Iproclaimed Emperor of Austria and King of Hungary, Croatia and Bohemia at Olmütz after the abdication of his uncle Ferdinand I
AbolitionistJohn Brownhanged for murder, treason, and conspiring slaves to revolt at Charles Town, Virginia
In a New York City theater, British authorCharles Dickensgives his first public reading in the United States
1st British government ofBenjamin Disraeliresigns
Camille Saint-Saëns' opera "Samson et Dalila" premieres in Weimar
Johannes Brahms' "3rd Symphony in F" premieres with the Vienna Philharmonic Orchestra
Gerhart Hauptmann's play "Die versunkene Glocke" premieres in Berlin
Exiled South African President of TransvaalPaul Krugerarrives in Germany, though the Kaiser refuses to meet him
American businessmanKing C. Gillettebegins selling safety razor blades
Canadian world heavyweight boxing championTommy Burnsretains his title; KOs Englishman Gunner Moir in 10th round at the National Sporting Club, Covent Garden, London
Vladimir Mayakovsky's self titled tragedy "Vladimir Mayakovsky" premieres at Saint Petersburg's Luna Park theatre

PaleoanthropologistDavidson Blackannounces to the Geological Society of China that ancient human fossils from Zhoukoudian, China are a new species which he names 'Sinanthropus Pekinensis' (Peking Man), now known as Homo Erectus
St. Louis Cardinals future Baseball HOF first basemanJim Bottomleyis voted NL MVP
American mobsterLouis Buchalteris sentenced to death along with his lieutenants Emanuel Weiss and Louis Capone
New York Giants nameMel Ottas player-manager; replaces another future Baseball Hall of FamerBill Terry, who heads Giants' farm system
World’s 1st self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction occurs inChicago Pile-1(world's 1st nuclear reactor) at the University of Chicago, overseen byEnrico FermiandLeó Szilárd
French GeneralCharles De Gaullearrives in Moscow, USSR

After one of the best seasons in baseball history, St Louis Cardinals utilityStan Musialis named NL MVP; leads NL in batting average (.365), runs (135), RBI (131), hits (230), doubles (46), triples (18) & slugging (.702), with 39 HRs
"I Robot" collection of sci-fi short stories byIsaac Asimovpublished by Gnome Press in the US
Future Pro Football Hall of Fame wide receiverDon Hutsonhas his #14 jersey retired by the Green Bay Packers; first number retired in franchise history
Fidel Castrolands with "Granma" on coast of Cuba
Sam Cooke's single "You Send Me" reaches #1
PaleoanthropologistLouis Leakeydiscovers 1.4 million year old Homo erectus (Olduvai Hominid 9) in Olduvai Gorge, Tanzania
Ringo Starr's tonsils are removed at University College Hospital, London, England
US PresidentRichard NixonnamesHenry Kissingersecurity advisor
Frank Robinsonis traded from the Baltimore Orioles to the Los Angeles Dodgers
Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyanis appointed President of the United Arab Emirates
Romanian tennis starIlie Năstasesuccessfully defends his ATP Masters Grand Prix title with a 6–3, 6–2, 3–6, 2–6, 6–3 win over AmericanStan Smithin Barcelona, Spain
Giant Baba beatsJack Briscoin Kagoshimi, to become NWA champ
Greg Chappellin his debut as Australian cricket captain completes a fine double in 8 wicket win in 1st Test v West Indies in Brisbane; scores 109no in 2nd innings to follow his 1st innings 123
Neil Diamond&Barbra Streisand's duet single "You Don't Bring Me Flowers" hits #1
PitcherFernando Valenzuelabecomes 3rd consecutive Los Angeles Dodgers player to be named National League Rookie of the Year, after Rick Sutcliffe (1979), and Steve Howe (1980)
Miami quarterbackDan Marinobreaks NFL single-season touchdown pass record when he throws his 37th in the Dolphins' 45-34 loss to the Raiders; finishes season with 48 TD passes

Houston Rockets score a 94-85 win over the Knicks at New York’s Madison Square Garden, recording their 15th straight win to start the season; tie Washington Capitols' all-time NBA record 1948-49; streak ends next game
"Cobb" a film about baseball playerTy Cobb, starringTommy Lee Jonespremieres

Montreal Canadiens coach Mario Tremblay leaves goaltenderPatrick Royin the game too long, allowing an embarrassing 9 goals in 11-1 rout by Detroit Red Wings; Roy demands trade; moves to Colorado Avalanche and wins Stanley Cup that season
Tony Fernandes' company Tune Air Sdn Bhd buys the heavily indebted AirAsia for 1 MYR (about USD 0.26 at the time)
US reality TV show "The Simple Life" withParis Hiltonand Nicole Richie premieres on Fox-TV
Ballon d'Or: Manchester United's Portuguese forwardCristiano Ronaldowins his first award as best football player in the world; Barcelona forwardLionel Messi2nd, Liverpool striker Fernando Torres 3rd
"The Hobbit: The Desolation of Smaug", 2nd film in the Hobbit series, directed byPeter Jackson, starringMartin FreemanandIan McKellen, premieres in Los Angeles
ComedianBill Cosbyresigns from the board of trustees of an American university following renewed sexual assault allegations
Elizabeth Lauten, an aide to a Republican congressman resigns after her online criticism of PresidentBarack Obama's teenage daughters
Stephen Hawkingclaims that Artificial Intelligence could be a "threat to mankind" and spell the end of the human race
Donald Trumptakes a call with Taiwanese leaderTsai Ing-wen, in a break from America's long standing "One China" policy
Andrés Manuel López Obradoris sworn in as the 65th President of Mexico
Israeli police recommend Prime MinisterBenjamin Netanyahuand his wife be charged with fraud and bribery
Nigerian PresidentMuhammadu Buharidenies he has died and been replaced by a Sudanese impostor after ill health
Trade war truce agreed between US PresidentDonald Trumpand Chinese PresidentXi Jinpingat G-20 meeting in Argentina
Adut Akech, Sudanese-Australian model and activist, wins 'Model of the Year' award at the British Fashion Awards in London
FIFA Ballon d'Or: Barcelona forwardLionel Messiwins his record 6th award from Liverpool's Dutch defender Virgil van Dijk; American midfielderMegan Rapinoeis Women's World Player of the Year
US PresidentJoe Bidenlands in Luanda, Angola, on his only trip to sub-Saharan Africa, the first US president to visit since 2015
GeneralNapoleon Bonaparteis crowned Emperor of the French at the Notre Dame de Paris in a ceremony officiated by Pope Pius VII
Japanese Marshal AdmiralYamamotosends his fleet to Pearl Harbor
Agostino Agazzari, Italian organist, composer of madrigals and sacred music, maestro di cappella (Siena Cathedral, 1607-40), and music theorist, born in Siena, Duchy of Florence (d. 1640)
Nicolaas Kruik, Dutch hydraulic engineer (drained Haarlemmermeer), born in West-Vlieland, Netherlands (d. 1754)

William Shirley, British Army officer (King George's War; French and Indian War), and colonial administrator (Governor of the Province of Massachusetts Bay, 1741-49 and 1753-56; Governor of the Bahamas, 1760–68), born in Preston, Sussex, England (d. 1771)
Ferdinand Konščak (aka Fernando Consag), Croatian explorer, cartographer (Baja California), and Jesuit missionary, born in Varaždin, Kingdom of Croatia (d. 1759)
Marianus Königsperger, German composer, organist, and Benedictine monk, born in Roding, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1769)
Carlo Bertinazzi, Italian actor and writer (commedia dell'arte), born in Turin, Italy (d. 1783)
Ferdinando Galiani, Italian economist (Della Moneta), born in Chieti, Italy (d. 1787)
William Cooper, American judge and developer (founded Cooperstown, New York), born in Smithfield, Pennsylvania (d. 1809)
John Breckinridge, American lawyer (5th United States Attorney General) and politician (U.S. Senator from Kentucky), born in Augusta County, Virginia (d. 1806)
Joseph Graetz, German organist, composer, and music educator, born in Vohburg on the Danube, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1826)
Henry Doeff, Dutch trader in Japan and translator who wrote a Dutch-Japanese dictionary, born in Amsterdam, Dutch Republic (d. 1835)
Melancthon Smith Wade, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1868)
Ahmad Pasha Bey, 10th Husainid Bey of Tunis, (1837–55) who abolished slavery, born in Tunis (d. 1855)
Jean-Charles Chapais, Canadian politician (considered Father of the Canadian Confederation), born in Rivière-Ouelle, Lower Canada (d. 1885)
Jacob Rosenhaim, Jewish-German composer, born in Mannheim, Grand Duchy of Baden (d. 1894)
Heinrich von Sybel, German historian (Geschichte des ersten Kreuzzugs), born in Düsseldorf, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1895)
George Scharf, British art critic and administrator (National Portrait Gallery, 1857-95), born in London, England (d. 1895)
Rufus Barringer, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Cabarrus County, North Carolina (d. 1895)
Pedro II, Emperor of Brazil (1831-89), born in Palace of São Cristóvão, Rio de Janeiro, Empire of Brazil (d. 1891)
Charles Garrison Harker, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Swedesboro, New Jersey (d. 1864)
Pierre Waldeck-Rousseau, French politician (29th Prime Minister of France), born in Nantes, France (d. 1904)
Deacon White, American Baseball Hall of Fame catcher (NL batting champion, RBI leader 1877; Boston Red Stockings), born in Caton, New York (d. 1939)
Robert Kajanus, Finnish conductor and composer, born in Helsingfors, Finland, Russian Empire (d. 1933)
Charles Studd, English cricket all-rounder (5 Tests; Middlesex) and missionary (Cambridge Seven), born in Spratton, England (d. 1931)
Charles Ringling, American circus owner (Ringling Brothers and Barnum & Bailey Circus), born in McGregor, Iowa (d. 1926)
Gustav Wohlgemuth, German composer, born in Leipzig, Kingdom of Saxony (d. 1937)
Francis Jammes, French poet and writer (Jammisme), born in Tournay, Hautes-Pyrénées, France (d. 1938)
Jonas Cohn, German philosopher (Theory of the Dialects), born in Görlitz, Germany (d. 1947)
Francis Casadesus, French violinist, composer and conductor, born in Paris (d. 1954)
Frank Reicher, German-American actor (King Kong, Son of Kong), born in Munich, Germany (d. 1965)
Ruth Draper, American actress and dramatist (The Italian Lesson), born in New York City (d. 1956)
George Minot, American physician (Nobel 1934 - introduction of a raw-liver diet in the treatment of pernicious anemia), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1950)
Earl Cooper, American auto racer (AAA National Championship 1913, 15, 17; Indianapolis 500, 1924 runner-up), born in Broken Bow, Nebraska (d. 1965)
William Gaxton [Arthur Gaxiola], American stage and screen actor (A Connecticut Yankee; Destry; Convoy), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1963)
Fate Marable, American jazz pianist, riverboat calliope player, and bandleader, born in Paducah, Kentucky (d. 1947)
Charles H. Wesley, American author and historian (The History of Alpha Phi Alpha), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1987)
Leo Ornstein, Russian-American composer (Bio in Sonata Form), born in Kremenchuk, Russia (d. 2002) (birth date uncertain)
Louis Freeman, Scottish bandleader, born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1994)
Warren William [Krech], American actor (The Wolf Man, Cleopatra), born in Aitkin, Minnesota (d. 1948)
Harriet Cohen, British pianist, born in London (d. 1967)
Jesse Crawford, American pianist, theatre organist for silent films, and recording artist, born in Woodland, California (d. 1962)
Klaas Voskuil, Dutch journalist, born in Zwolle, Netherlands (d. 1975)
Hovhannes Bagramyan, Soviet military commander and Marshal of the Soviet Union of Armenian origin, born in Yelizavetpol, Elisabethpol Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1982)
Rewi Alley, New Zealand labor and peace activist based in China and writer (Americans in China), born in Springfield, Canterbury, New Zealand (d. 1987)
Indra Lal Roy, Indian World War I flying ace, born in Calcutta, British India (d. 1918)
John Barbirolli, English conductor (NY Philharmonic Orchestra), born in Southampton Row, Holborn, London (d. 1970)
John Cobb, British auto racer (World Land Speed Record 1938-39, 47), born in Esher, Surrey, England (d. 1952)
(Gustaaf) "Staf" Nees, Flemish-Belgian carillonist, organist, composer (Maria-oratorio), and educator, born in Mechelen, Belgium (d. 1965)
Raimundo Orsi, Argentine-born Italian soccer winger (13 caps Argentina, 35 Italy; Independiente, Juventus), born in (d. 1986)
Horace Hildreth, American politician (Gov-Maine, 1945-49) and U.S. Ambassador to Pakistan (1953-57), born in Gardiner, Maine (d. 1988)
Miroslav Ponc, Czech composer, born in Vysoké Mýto (d. 1976)
Moses Asch, Polish-American recording engineer (founder of Folkways Records), born in Warsaw, Poland, Russian Empire (d. 1986)[1]
Osvaldo Pugliese, Argentine musician and composer, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1995)
Donald Woods, Canadian actor (True Grit, Craig Kennedy; Criminologist), born in Brandon, Manitoba (d. 1998)
Eric Dalton, South African cricket batsman (15 Tests, top score 117), born in Durban, Natal, South Africa (d. 1981)
Hy Gardner, American newspaper columnist (New York Herald Tribune, 1951-66), and television panelist, born in New York City (d. 1989)
Robert F. Simon, American actor (The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance; Operation Petticoat; M*A*S*H), born in Mansfield, Ohio (d. 1992)
Rufus "Rufe" Davis, American actor (Cocoanut Grove, Trail Blazers, Gangs of Sonora), born in Vinson, Oklahoma (d. 1974)
Bob Newson, South African cricket fast bowler (3 Tests, 4 wickets; Transvaal; Rhodesia), born in Sea Point, Cape Province, South Africa (d. 1988)
Robert Paige, American actor, TV newscaster and political correspondent (Son of Dracula, Pardon My Sarong, Colgate Comedy Hour), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1987)
Russell Lynes, American art historian, photographer, author and managing editor of Harper's Magazine, born in Great Barrington, Massachusetts (d. 1991)
Taisto Mäki, Finnish athlete (WR 2 miles, 5,000m and 10,000m simultaneously 1939-42), born in Rekola, Finland (d. 1979)
Eddie Sauter, American big band and theatrical arranger, bandleader (Sauter-Finegan Orchestra), and jazz composer (Stan Getz -"Focus"), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 1981)
Nico Richter, Dutch composer, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1945)
Randolph Apperson Hearst, American newspaper publisher (Hearst Newspapers), born in New York City (d. 2000)
Charlie Ventura [Venturo], American jazz tenor saxophonist and bandleader (Bop for the People), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1992)
John Bentley, British actor (African Patrol, Crossroads), born in Birmingham, England (d. 2009)
Ezra Stone [Chaim Feinstone], American director, actor and producer (My Living Doll, Henry Aldrich), born in New Bedford, Massachusetts (d. 1994)
Sylvia Syms, American jazz singer (Hello Dolly; Dream Girl; Them There Eyes), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 1992)

Milton Delugg, American pianist, accordion player, composer ("Hoop-Dee-Do"; "Santa Claus Conquers The Martians"), and orchestra leader (The Tonight Show, 1966; The Paul Winchell Show; The Gong Show), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2015)[1]
Robert Moevs, American composer, born in La Crosse, Wisconsin (d. 2007)
Isabella Karle, American scientist who was instrumental in developing techniques to extract plutonium chloride, born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2017)
Leo Gordon, American actor (Maverick, Circus Boy, Enos, Winds of War), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2000)
Meshulam Riklis, Turkish-born Israeli businessman and husband of Pia Zadora, born in Istanbul, Turkey (d. 2019)
John Herbert "Jonathan" Frid, Canadian actor (Barnabas Collins-Dark Shadows), born in Hamilton, Ontario, Canada (d. 2012)
Carla Del Poggio [Maria Luisa Attanasio], Italian stage and screen actress (Variety Lights; Immortal Melodies), born in Naples, Kingdom of Italy (d. 2010)
Dee Carroll [Betty Jeanne Marsh], American actress (Mad Bombe; Split), born in Denver, Colorado (d. 1980)
Julie Harris, American actress (The Bell Jar: I Am A Camera; East of Eden), born in Grosse Pointe, Michigan (d. 2013)
Hobie Billingsley, American ISHOF diving coach (Indiana University 1959-89; 6 x NCAA team championships; US Olympic team 1968, 72, 76), born in Erie, Pennsylvania (d. 2022)[1]
Jim David, American NFL defensive back (Pro Bowl 1954-59; NFL Champion 1952-53, 57; Detroit Lions), born in Florence, South Carolina (d. 2007)
Ralph Beard, American basketball guard (Olympic gold 1948; NBA All-Star 1951; life ban point shaving scandal 1951), born in Hardinsburg, Kentucky (d. 2007)
